did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), specifically a fine-tuned VGG16 model, for multi-class classification of skin lesions (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevi, Basal Cell Carcinoma) using the HAM10000 and ISIC datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a fine-tuned VGG16 model for skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 and ISIC datasets. It includes specific sections on methodology (dataset description, preprocessing, model architecture, training parameters), results (accuracy, precision, recall, confusion matrices), and discussion of findings. Although it contains a 'Related Work' section, its primary purpose is reporting new empirical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Cross-Dataset Dermatology Framework: HAM10000 Training with ISIC Testing
journal: 2026 International Conference on Computing, Sciences and Communications (ICCSC)
brief_summary: This study developed a cross-dataset dermatology framework using a fine-tuned VGG16 convolutional neural network for multi-class classification of Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevi, and Basal Cell Carcinoma. The model was trained on a balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset and evaluated on a combined test set comprising 15% of HAM10000 and the ISIC 2019 dataset. To address class imbalance and overfitting, the researchers employed under-sampling, data augmentation, and optimization strategies. The final model achieved an accuracy of 84.2% on the HAM10000 validation set and 76.5% on the combined ISIC test set. These results highlight the challenges of dataset diversity and the importance of preprocessing for effective skin lesion classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes used in the multi-class classification task. The model achieved a recall of 0.71 for this class.
One of the three classes used in the multi-class classification task. The model achieved a precision of 0.71 and recall of 0.68 for this class.
One of the three classes used in the multi-class classification task. The model achieved a precision of 0.76 and F1-score of 0.73 for this class.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions: Melanoma (mel), Melanocytic Nevi (nv), and Basal Cell Carcinoma (bkl).
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a multi-class classification task using a fine-tuned VGG16 model. The model outputs probabilities for three distinct classes via a SoftMax output layer with three units. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and a 3x3 confusion matrix for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A publicly available collection of dermoscopic images sourced from Kaggle, developed by Tschandl et al. in 2018, comprising over 10,000 high-resolution dermoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions collected from various medical institutions.

use.training: Used for training the VGG16 model. A balanced subset of 3,297 images (1,099 per class) was created via under-sampling, with 70% (2,307 images) allocated to the training set.
use.validation: 15% of the balanced HAM10000 subset (495 images) was used for validation.
use.testing: 15% of the original HAM10000 dataset (1,337 images) was included in the test set to assess performance across data distributions.
use.other: Used to create a balanced training subset via under-sampling.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration dataset, a well-known standard for skin cancer discovery containing images of various benign and malignant skin lesions.

use.testing: Used as part of the combined test set to evaluate the model's generalizability across different data sources.
use.other: Used for external comparison and evaluation of cross-dataset performance.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess classification performance, calculate precision, recall, and F1-score for each class, and analyze misclassifications between classes (e.g., Melanocytic Nevi vs. Melanoma).
To ensure balanced class distribution across training, validation, and test sets, enabling consistent performance assessment.
To compute macro-averaged metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) that account for class support, validating balanced performance.
To prevent overfitting by monitoring validation loss and stopping training if no improvement occurs for 10 epochs.
To aid fine-tuning by reducing the learning rate by a factor of 0.1 if validation loss plateaus for 5 epochs.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

validation_set.notes: The model achieved 84.2% accuracy on the HAM10000 validation set (495 images).

test_set_subset.notes: The model achieved 70% accuracy on the 15% subset of HAM10000 included in the test set (495 images, 165 per class).

test_set_combined.notes: The model achieved 76.5% accuracy on the combined test set comprising the ISIC 2019 dataset and 15% of HAM10000.

Combined_Test_Set.description: Composed of 15% of HAM10000 and the ISIC 2019 dataset

Melanoma (mel).notes: Precision calculated as 117/(117+55). 71% of real melanoma cases were successfully detected.

Melanocytic Nevi (nv).notes: Precision calculated as 110/(110+55). Recall calculated as 110/165.

Basal Cell Carcinoma (bkl).notes: Highest precision and F1-score among classes. 30% of actual bkl cases were missed.

Combined_Test_Set.notes: Initial trials with a VGG16-grounded model revealed overfitting issues, reducing test accuracy from 70% to 62% before optimization strategies were enforced. The final model achieved 70% accuracy on the HAM10000 test subset and 76.5% on the combined ISIC test set.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet dataset (1 million images, 1,000 categories)

architecture_details.convolutional_layers: 13 layers divided into five blocks, each followed by max-pooling layers
architecture_details.fully_connected_layers: Original 3 fully connected layers replaced with custom layers
custom_layers: Flattening Layer (converts 2D feature maps to 1D vector)
Dense Layers (added to acquire task-specific features)
SoftMax Output Layer (3 units for classes: mel, nv, bkl)
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Convolutional base kept to extract low-level and high-level patterns; initially frozen, then fine-tuned by unfreezing some convolutional layers

callbacks: Model Checkpoint (saves best weights based on validation accuracy)
Reduce LROn Plateau (reduces learning rate by factor of 0.1 if validation loss plateaus for 5 epochs)
training_details.training_phase: Initially trained only custom layers with frozen convolutional base; later fine-tuned by unfreezing some convolutional layers

classification_or_output_head.type: Custom classification head replacing original fully connected layers

preprocessing_techniques: Image Resizing: Images were resized to 224 x 224 pixels to meet VGG16 model requirements.
Normalization: RGB images were rescaled to the 0–1 range by dividing pixel values by 255 to accelerate model convergence.

Data Augmentation (Training Set Only): Applied to improve robustness and avoid overfitting, including: Rotation (30 degrees), Width and Height Shifts (up to 0.2), Shear transformation (0.2), Random Zoom (0.2), Horizontal Flips, and Brightness Adjustments (variations between 0.8 and 1.2).
Class Balancing via Under-sampling: A balanced subset of 3,297 images (1,099 per class) was created from the HAM10000 dataset to address class imbalance.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated at an angle of 30 degrees.
Images were shifted up to 0.2 in both directions.
A shear transformation of 0.2 was applied to mimic slight perspective distortions.
Random zooming by 0.2 was applied to address variations in lesion size or camera distance.
Left-to-right flips were applied, leveraging lesion symmetry.
Brightness variations between 0.8 and 1.2 were applied to simulate diverse lighting conditions.

conclusions: The fine-tuned VGG16 model achieved a test accuracy of 70% on the HAM10000 dataset and 76.5% on the combined ISIC and HAM10000 test set, demonstrating moderate generalizability across different data sources.
The model achieved a melanoma recall of 0.71, indicating its effectiveness in detecting malignant cases, which is critical for early detection.

The Basal Cell Carcinoma (bkl) class achieved the highest precision (0.76) and F1-score (0.73), while the Melanocytic Nevi (nv) class showed balanced performance with a precision of 0.71 and recall of 0.68.

The model struggled to distinguish between Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevi, resulting in a high false-positive rate for melanoma (precision of 0.64), which raises concerns about unnecessary biopsies in clinical settings.

Initial trials with a VGG16-grounded model revealed overfitting issues, reducing test accuracy from 70% to 62%, which was addressed through optimization strategies including under-sampling, data augmentation, and learning rate reduction.
Dataset diversity, varying image quality, and class imbalance pose significant challenges to model performance, highlighting the importance of careful preprocessing and architectural advancements.
Future work should explore advanced models, ensemble methods, and domain adaptation approaches to minimize biases and further improve performance for real-world clinical operations.
important_remarks: The study employs a cross-dataset evaluation strategy (training on HAM10000, testing on a combination of HAM10000 and ISIC 2019), which highlights significant challenges in generalizability due to dataset diversity and varying image quality.

The model achieved a melanoma recall of 0.71, but suffered from low precision (0.64) for melanoma, resulting in a high false-positive rate where 37 cases of Melanocytic Nevi were erroneously classified as Melanoma, raising concerns about unnecessary biopsies in clinical settings.

Initial trials with the VGG16 model revealed severe overfitting, causing test accuracy to drop from 70% to 62%, which was subsequently mitigated through under-sampling, data augmentation, and optimization strategies.

The training dataset was artificially balanced via under-sampling (reducing the dataset to 3,297 images) to address class imbalance, which may limit the model's exposure to the full variance of the original HAM10000 distribution.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values or confidence intervals); performance is evaluated solely using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices.

The test set includes 15% of the HAM10000 dataset alongside the ISIC 2019 dataset, which serves as an external validation source but introduces heterogeneity in lesion characteristics and background noise.

The study acknowledges that the limited size of the balanced training set (3,297 images) and residual class imbalance effects likely contribute to overfitting, as evidenced by the gap between training (82%) and test (70%) accuracy.

The model's performance on Basal Cell Carcinoma (bkl) was superior (precision 0.76, F1-score 0.73) compared to Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevi, indicating potential bias or difficulty in distinguishing between melanocytic lesions.
